title: Cat Regurgitating Hairballs: Veterinary Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Understand cat hairball regurgitation. Learn why it happens, when to see a vet, and why photos help with triage. Urgency level: Low.
content_html:
What is Hairball Regurgitation?

Hairball regurgitation, medically known as trichobezoars, occurs when a cat swallows fur while grooming. Because fur is indigestible, it can sometimes accumulate in the stomach, forming a cylindrical mass. To clear this, the cat expels the mass through the esophagus. It is common for the process to be accompanied by a hacking or retching sound.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for a cat regurgitating a hairball is generally Low. Most cats will expel the hairball and immediately return to their normal activities, such as eating or playing. However, if your cat is repeatedly retching without producing anything, appears lethargic, or refuses food for more than 24 hours, it could indicate a blockage, which requires a veterinary visit.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you are unsure whether your cat is experiencing a normal hairball or a more serious digestive issue, taking a clear photo of the expelled material is extremely helpful. A veterinarian can use the photo to differentiate between fur, undigested food (regurgitation), or bile (vomiting). This visual evidence helps determine if the issue is a simple grooming byproduct or something requiring diagnostic testing.

faqs.question: How often is it normal for a cat to have a hairball?
faqs.answer: It is generally considered normal for a cat to produce a hairball once every week or two. If it happens more frequently, it may indicate over-grooming or a gastrointestinal motility issue.

faqs.question: What is the difference between vomiting and regurgitation?
faqs.answer: Regurgitation is a passive process where undigested contents come up from the esophagus, often shaped like a tube. Vomiting is an active process involving abdominal contractions and often contains partially digested food and yellow bile.

faqs.question: Can I help my cat pass hairballs more easily?
faqs.answer: Yes, regular brushing to remove loose fur and using veterinarian-approved hairball lubricants or specialized high-fiber diets can help reduce hairball formation.
